q: What is the difference between a fact table and a dimension table? Give me a concrete example.
a: A fact table stores measurable events at a declared grain, holding numeric additive measures like quantity and revenue plus foreign keys to dimensions (e.g. fct_order_items). A dimension table stores the descriptive context around those facts, the who/what/when/where, like dim_customer with name and segment or dim_date with month and quarter. The grain rule is paramount: each fact row represents exactly one measurable event at the declared grain. If you sum it, it belongs in a fact; if you filter or group by it, it belongs in a dimension, and in a star schema the central fact joins outward to dimensions via foreign keys.
fj: Facts have numbers and dimensions have text — that's basically it.
fs: Facts store additive measures at a declared grain; dimensions provide descriptive context for filtering and grouping. The grain determines which columns belong where.

q: Explain normalization: what does 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF mean, and when would you normalize versus denormalize?
a: 1NF requires atomic values with no repeating groups; 2NF adds full dependence of every non-key column on the entire primary key (no partial dependencies, relevant only with composite keys); 3NF adds no transitive dependencies, so non-key columns depend only on the primary key and not on each other. Normalize to 3NF for OLTP systems where data integrity and avoiding update anomalies matter most. Denormalize for analytics/OLAP, where dimensional models intentionally embed redundancy (e.g. city, state, and country together in dim_geography) to minimize joins. It is a deliberate workload-driven trade-off, not a universal correctness rule.
fj: 3NF is always best; you should always normalize.
fs: 3NF is best for OLTP integrity; for analytics we intentionally denormalize into star schemas to minimize join complexity.

q: What is a surrogate key and why do you use one in a dimensional model instead of the natural business key?
a: A surrogate key is a system-generated, meaningless identifier (usually an integer or hash) assigned by the warehouse, whereas a natural key is a real source-system identifier like a CRM ID or email. Surrogate keys provide source independence (stable even if the source rekeys after a merger), enable SCD Type 2 versioning (the same entity gets multiple dimension rows, each with its own key, while the fact stores the version valid at event time), and join faster than long strings. The most important reason is SCD2: storing the natural key would always return today's attributes rather than the point-in-time values. Always still carry the natural key in the dimension so you can map back to the source.
fj: I'd just use the customer_id from the source system as the key.
fs: Natural keys break SCD Type 2 versioning and are vulnerable to source-system rekeying. Surrogate keys decouple the warehouse from source instability.

q: SCD Type 1 vs Type 2 vs Type 3 — how do you choose which to apply for a given attribute?
a: Type 1 overwrites in place with no history, suitable when history doesn't matter (like fixing a typo). Type 2 inserts a new dimension row with effective_from, effective_to, and is_current columns, used when historical reporting must reflect the attribute's value at event time (like customer segment at order time). Type 3 adds a previous_value column alongside the current one and is rarely used because it tracks only a single prior state. The decision driver is a business question: does a report on last year's orders need the attribute as it was then (Type 2) or as it is now (Type 1)?
fj: I'd always use Type 2 to be safe.
fs: Type 2 has real costs — dimension growth, complex joins. I ask the business whether they need point-in-time accuracy before committing to Type 2 for every attribute.

q: When would you use a snowflake schema instead of a star schema, and what are the trade-offs?
a: A star schema fully denormalizes dimensions so there is one join per dimension, making it simpler and faster for BI; a snowflake schema normalizes dimensions into sub-dimensions, reducing storage for large high-redundancy dimensions and supporting deep hierarchies but at the cost of more joins and harder queries. Use snowflake only when dimensions are very large with many repeated values, storage constraints are real, or a hierarchy has many levels you must navigate dynamically. The modern default is the star schema, because columnar warehouses compress repeated values cheaply and largely eliminate the storage argument for snowflaking. Note the snowflake schema pattern is unrelated to the Snowflake database product.
fj: Snowflake schema is better because it's more normalized.
fs: Normalized is better for OLTP integrity, not for analytics performance. Star schema wins on query simplicity, and columnar compression mostly eliminates the storage argument for snowflaking.

q: Compare a normalized (3NF) schema to a dimensional (star) schema. When would you use each?
a: A 3NF schema minimizes redundancy, enforces referential integrity, and optimizes for writes, but requires many tables and joins. A star schema is intentionally denormalized into fact and dimension tables, accepting redundancy for fast aggregation reads, fewer joins, and easy BI-tool use. Use 3NF when transactional integrity and high write throughput matter (OLTP); use a star schema when the system is analytics-facing and reads far outnumber writes. The modern reality is that most platforms use both: 3NF in the source/operational layer and a star schema in the presentation/mart layer.
fj: 3NF is for good databases; star schema is a shortcut.
fs: They optimize for different workloads. 3NF is correct for OLTP write integrity; star schema is correct for OLAP read performance. Most platforms use both in different layers.

q: Walk me through designing the grain for a sales orders fact table. What happens if you get the grain wrong?
a: Declare the grain first, out loud, as something like 'one row per order line item', then follow Kimball's four steps: pick the business process, declare the grain, identify dimensions, and identify facts. Line-item grain is chosen over order grain because order-level loses the ability to analyze per-item revenue, discounts, and category breakdowns. Getting the grain wrong produces mixed-grain fact tables, wrong aggregations from summing measures that aren't additive at that grain, and joins that fan out unexpectedly. Every column must be consistent with the declared grain; for example, total_order_value repeats on every line of an order and would triple-count if summed at line-item grain.
fj: I'd put total_order_value in the line-item fact table.
fs: total_order_value repeats on every line item of the same order — summing it at line-item grain triple-counts the order. That's a grain violation; it belongs in an order-level fact.

code.label: triple-counts the order
lines: -- grain: one row per order line
-- repeats on every line of an

q: Explain additive, semi-additive, and non-additive measures. Give an example of each and explain how you handle non-additive measures in a fact table.
a: Additive measures (revenue, quantity, cost) can be summed across all dimensions including time. Semi-additive measures (account_balance, inventory_level) can be summed across some dimensions but not time, so use MAX or AVG across time rather than SUM. Non-additive measures (conversion_rate, profit_margin, ratios) cannot be meaningfully summed across any dimension. To handle non-additive measures, store the additive components (numerator and denominator) as facts and compute the ratio at query time, never storing only the ratio itself.
fj: I'd store conversion_rate directly in the fact table as a measure.
fs: Conversion_rate is non-additive — storing only the ratio breaks all roll-ups. Store sessions and conversions as additive facts; derive the rate at query time.

lines: -- derive the rate at query time

q: What are the three types of fact tables — transaction, periodic snapshot, and accumulating snapshot? When would you use each?
a: A transaction fact has one row per discrete event (a sale, click, or ticket) and is the most common type, used to analyze frequency and individual event attributes. A periodic snapshot has one row per entity per time period capturing state at intervals (one row per account per month-end), used for slowly changing quantities like balance or inventory, and its measures are semi-additive. An accumulating snapshot has one row per business process instance, updated in place as it moves through stages (order_placed, shipped, delivered), used for processes with a defined start, milestones, and end. The signal: event grain means transaction, (entity, period) grain means periodic snapshot, and a mutable process instance means accumulating snapshot.
fj: All fact tables are the same — they store events.
fs: Transaction facts store individual events (append-only). Periodic snapshots store entity state per period (semi-additive, scheduled rows). Accumulating snapshots store process instances updated in place as milestones complete.

q: How do you choose between a star schema, snowflake schema, and One Big Table for a new analytics mart?
a: A star schema is the general-purpose default: one join per dimension, BI-friendly, and flexible as questions change. A snowflake schema normalizes dimensions and is chosen only for very large dimensions with deep hierarchies or real storage constraints. One Big Table pre-joins all dimensions and measures into one wide table for maximum read speed on a known, stable query pattern, accepting rigidity. The decision: general-purpose layer means star; one dominant low-latency query means OBT; very deep hierarchies mean selective snowflake. Many teams build a star schema as the authoritative layer and materialize OBT feature tables for specific high-traffic consumers.
fj: OBT is bad practice — always use a star schema.
fs: OBT is the right call for a specific high-traffic known query pattern. Star schema is the general-purpose default; OBT is a targeted read optimization.

q: How do you decide which attributes of a dimension require SCD Type 2 tracking vs. Type 1 overwrite?
a: Ask the business question: if a report runs tomorrow on last year's orders, should the attribute show its value at the time of the order (Type 2) or today's value (Type 1)? Use Type 2 for business-critical attributes like customer tier, sales rep territory, product category, and compliance-sensitive fields; use Type 1 for correctable errors, current-only contact info, and meaningless technical IDs. Most production dimensions are hybrid, applying Type 1 to some attributes and Type 2 to others on the same table. Apply Type 2 surgically because each Type 2 attribute forces temporal range joins and grows the dimension.
fj: I'd Type 2 every attribute to be safe.
fs: Type 2 has real costs — dimension growth and range-join complexity. I apply it surgically to attributes where point-in-time accuracy materially changes a reported number, based on a business requirements conversation.

q: SCD Type 1 vs. SCD Type 2 — compare them on implementation complexity, storage cost, and join pattern. When does each give a wrong answer if misapplied?
a: Type 1 is a simple in-place UPDATE with no dimension growth and a plain surrogate-key equality join, but it gives wrong answers when history matters because a report on 'tier at time of purchase' returns today's tier. Type 2 inserts a new row and closes the old one via MERGE logic, growing the dimension and requiring a temporal range join (event_date BETWEEN effective_from AND effective_to), and it gives wrong answers if you join on the natural key with is_current = 1, which always returns today's attributes and defeats versioning. The correct Type 2 join uses the surrogate key stamped at event time. The misapplication failures are silent, producing plausible but wrong numbers with no error.
fj: For SCD2 I just join on customer_id and filter is_current = 1.
fs: That always returns today's attributes — it defeats Type 2 entirely. The correct join uses the surrogate key stamped at event time, or a temporal range join on the event date.

lines: -- on the fact at event time

q: Walk me through SCD Type 2 end to end — from source system change detection to a correct point-in-time query on the fact table. What are the failure modes at each step?
a: First, detect changes by comparing source rows to current dimension rows via a timestamp or a column-level hash (hash defends against unreliable updated_at). Second, close the current row and insert a new version using an atomic MERGE to avoid race conditions. Third, at fact load time stamp the fact with the surrogate key active at the event timestamp via a temporal range join. Fourth, query by a simple equality join on the stamped surrogate key, guarding against analysts re-joining on natural key plus is_current which silently returns today's attributes. Fifth, validate row counts and spot-check a known tier change. Note that dbt snapshots handle change detection and versioning but not the fact-stamping step.
fj: For SCD2, just add effective_from and effective_to and insert a new row when things change.
fs: The concept is right, but the failure modes are in the details: atomic MERGE to prevent race conditions, hash-based change detection when timestamps are unreliable, surrogate-key stamping at fact load time, and guarding against analysts joining on natural key + is_current which silently returns wrong historical attributes.

q: How do you model a many-to-many relationship in a star schema? Walk me through a bridge table implementation and the pitfalls.
a: Because a fact with multiple foreign keys to the same dimension breaks the grain and fans out, you introduce a bridge (intersection) table with one row per combination, such as bridge_student_course holding student_key, course_key, and optionally a weighting_factor. The fact joins to the bridge on one key, and the bridge resolves to the dimension, so fan-out happens at the bridge rather than the fact. The main pitfall is double-counting: summing a shared measure across the bridge counts it once per associated member, so add a weight column (e.g. 0.5/0.5 for two co-owners) and multiply the measure by weight before summing. When the relationship itself, not a measure, is the information, a factless fact table is the related pattern.
fj: I'd add a second product_key column to the fact table for multi-product orders.
fs: Multiple foreign key columns breaks the star schema grain and doesn't scale beyond 2 values. A bridge table with one row per (fact, dimension member) correctly handles M:N relationships.

q: How do you handle late-arriving dimensions in a data warehouse? Walk me through the patterns and their trade-offs.
a: Late-arriving dimensions occur when a fact arrives before its dimension record exists, so there is no surrogate key to stamp. Pattern 1 stores a NULL foreign key and backfills later, which is simplest but temporarily breaks reports. Pattern 2 uses an Unknown member placeholder row (surrogate key -1) that all late facts join to, then re-stamps them when the real row arrives, keeping reports working but polluting counts until backfilled. Pattern 3 creates an inferred dimension row with minimal attributes flagged as inferred, later updated via Type 1 overwrite while preserving the surrogate key. Whatever the pattern, you must run a scheduled, idempotent backfill job to resolve placeholders as dimensions load.
fj: I'd just wait for the dimension to load before loading facts.
fs: Blocking fact loading on dimension availability doesn't work at scale — facts arrive continuously. The right pattern is an unknown placeholder with a backfill job, or inferred dimension rows with Type 1 resolution on arrival.

q: Compare normalized (3NF), dimensional (star), and One Big Table modeling approaches for an analytics platform. When does each break down at scale?
a: 3NF gives write integrity but breaks for analytics when queries need 5-10+ joins that analysts can't sustain. A star schema is the analytics standard but breaks when dimensions become very numerous, when adding a dimension forces a costly fact backfill, or when users always need the same pre-joined shape. One Big Table maximizes read speed but breaks when schemas drift across hundreds of columns or when many distinct query patterns each demand a different OBT. The real architecture at scale rejects a single winner: 3NF in the source layer, star schema in the curated mart layer, and OBT as read-optimized derivatives, with columnar storage making wide OBTs more viable than in the row-store era.
fj: Star schema is always the right approach for analytics.
fs: Star schema is the right default for the curated mart layer, but real platforms use 3NF at the source, star in the mart, and OBT for specific high-traffic consumers. The architecture has layers, not one winning pattern.

q: You need to model a process that tracks order fulfilment through 5 stages. Compare transaction fact, periodic snapshot, and accumulating snapshot for this use case — which do you choose and why?
a: A transaction fact gives one row per stage transition (5 rows per order), good for event-level questions but requiring a self-join to compute end-to-end duration. A periodic snapshot gives one row per order per period, good for 'how many orders were at stage 3 on a date' but awkward for stage-to-stage durations. The winner is the accumulating snapshot: one row per order with stage_1_date through stage_5_date, updated in place, so 'time from stage 1 to stage 5' is a single DATEDIFF and bottlenecks are obvious. The operational cost is that rows are mutable and require MERGE/upsert ETL, and a strong combined approach pairs the accumulating snapshot with a transaction fact for real-time event monitoring.
fj: I'd use a transaction fact — simpler ETL.
fs: Transaction facts make the ETL simpler but make the analysis harder — answering 'time from stage 1 to stage 5' requires a pivot or self-join across 5 rows. Accumulating snapshot puts that into a single DATEDIFF on one row per order.

q: How do you decide between surrogate keys (integer / hash) and natural keys in a dimensional model — and when can natural keys be used in fact tables safely?
a: Always use surrogate keys in dimensions to decouple from source-key instability, enable SCD Type 2 versioning, and support cross-source entity resolution. Natural keys can live in facts as degenerate dimensions when the key (like an order_number) is itself at the fact grain, needing no separate dimension table or join. Integer surrogates are compact, fast, and sortable; hash surrogates (MD5/SHA of the natural key, as dbt uses) are deterministic and idempotent but larger and unsortable. Natural keys are safe in facts only when they are stable, unique, non-null, and the referenced entity has no SCD2, which is rare in practice.
fj: I'd use the source customer_id directly as the fact's foreign key.
fs: That breaks if the source rekeys, and doesn't support SCD Type 2 versioning. I use surrogate keys in dimensions; only natural keys that are at the fact grain and stable live directly in the fact as degenerate dimensions.

q: Star schema vs. 3NF vs. One Big Table — compare all three on read performance, write complexity, schema flexibility, and BI-tool compatibility.
a: On read performance, OBT is excellent (no joins), star is good (one join per dimension), and 3NF is poor (many joins). On write complexity and schema flexibility the order reverses: 3NF absorbs changes cheaply, star needs a new fact FK or backfill, and OBT must rebuild the entire wide table. For BI-tool compatibility the star schema is purpose-built, 3NF is poor, and OBT is simple but fragile on schema change. History tracking is native in star via SCD Type 1/2, hard in 3NF, and absent in OBT without expensive rebuilds. At scale you use all three in layers: 3NF at the source, star in the authoritative mart, OBT for read-optimized derivatives, with columnar storage making OBT more viable while star's flexibility keeps it the standard.
fj: Star schema is just better — 3NF and OBT are wrong.
fs: Each serves a different workload and layer. 3NF at the source, star in the mart, OBT for specific read-optimized derivatives. The architecture has layers.

q: What is a conformed dimension, and how does it enable drill-across reporting between two separate fact tables?
a: A conformed dimension is a single master dimension (like dim_date or dim_product) built once and shared by multiple fact tables, with identical surrogate keys, attribute names, and values across all of them. Because dim_product means exactly the same thing to fct_sales and fct_inventory, you can drill across: query each fact separately grouped by the shared dimension attribute, then merge the two result sets on that common attribute. The Kimball bus matrix is the planning artifact — fact processes as rows, conformed dimensions as columns — that guarantees the dimensions line up. Without conformance you cannot legitimately compare metrics across processes, because a 'product category' in one mart may not match the other.
fj: I'd just join the two fact tables together on product_id to combine sales and inventory.
fs: You never join two fact tables directly — that fans out into a wrong cross-product. You drill across: aggregate each fact to the conformed-dimension grain separately, then merge the results on the shared attribute.

q: Explain SCD Type 6 (the 1+2+3 hybrid). What problem does it solve that pure Type 2 cannot, and how do you query it?
a: Type 6 combines Type 1, 2, and 3 on the same dimension: it keeps Type 2 versioned rows with effective dating, but each row also carries a Type 1 'current' column that is overwritten on every version so it always reflects today's value. The added power over pure Type 2 is dual-perspective analysis from one join: stamp the fact with the surrogate key at event time, then select the historical (as-was) attribute or the current (as-is) attribute from the very same dimension row. For example a sales report can group by current_segment to see all of a customer's lifetime history rolled into their present segment, or by historical_segment to see the segment in effect at each order. Pure Type 2 forces an extra join or a Type 1 outrigger to get the as-is view.
fj: Type 6 is just Type 2 with more columns — there's no real difference.
fs: The point of Type 6 is the dual view from one row: historical_segment for as-was reporting and current_segment (Type 1, overwritten on every version) for as-is reporting, without a second join.

code.label: as-was vs as-is in one row

q: Explain Data Vault 2.0: what are hubs, links, and satellites, and why use them instead of a Kimball star schema for the integration layer?
a: A hub stores the distinct business keys for a concept (hub_customer holds customer_id plus a hash key and load metadata); a link stores relationships between hubs as their own table (link_order ties customer and product hubs); a satellite hangs off a hub or link and holds the descriptive, time-variant attributes, append-only with a load timestamp so every change is a new row. Data Vault 2.0 uses hash keys (MD5/SHA-256 of the business key) so loads are deterministic and parallelizable across many sources without a central sequence. You choose it over a star schema for the raw integration layer because it is insert-only and fully auditable, absorbs new sources and schema change without remodeling, and is highly parallel — then you still build Kimball star marts on top for consumption. Data Vault is not a replacement for star schemas; it sits underneath them.
fj: Data Vault is just another name for a star schema with different table names.
fs: Data Vault is an insert-only, hash-keyed integration layer optimized for auditability, parallel multi-source loads, and schema agility — not a query layer. You still build star-schema marts on top for BI.

q: What is a fan trap in a dimensional model, and how do you prevent the double-counting it causes in a BI tool?
a: A fan trap occurs when a query joins along a one-to-many chain and then aggregates a measure from the 'one' side: the join fans the parent rows out to the child grain, so summing the parent measure multiplies it by the number of children. Classic case: joining an order-grain measure to its order-line rows and summing the order total — it gets counted once per line. The fix is to never mix measures of different grain in one flat join-and-sum; instead aggregate each fact to its own grain in separate subqueries (or CTEs) and then join the pre-aggregated results, which is exactly the drill-across pattern. Modern BI semantic layers (LookML, dbt MetricFlow) detect this via declared fan-out relationships and switch to symmetric aggregates automatically.
fj: I'd just join everything into one big query and SUM the totals.
fs: Summing a coarser-grain measure across a one-to-many join multiplies it by the child count. Pre-aggregate each grain in its own subquery before joining, or rely on a semantic layer's symmetric aggregates.

lines: -- each is summed at its own grain

q: A customer dimension has demographic attributes (age band, income band, credit score band) that change frequently. Pure SCD Type 2 explodes the row count. How do you model this?
a: Use an SCD Type 4 mini-dimension: split the rapidly changing, low-cardinality banded attributes out of dim_customer into a separate dim_customer_demographics table whose grain is one row per unique combination of bands, not per customer. The fact carries two foreign keys — customer_key and demographic_key — so a change in band just points new facts at a different existing demographic row, capturing history in the fact without versioning the main dimension. This is why you band continuous values (income, score) into ranges: it caps the number of demographic combinations. Optionally add a Type 1 outrigger (sometimes called Type 5) holding the customer's current demographic_key on dim_customer for as-is profiling.
fj: I'd just SCD Type 2 the whole customer dimension including the demographic columns.
fs: Type 2 on rapidly changing attributes explodes the dimension — thousands of versions per customer. The mini-dimension (Type 4) holds banded combinations at their own grain and the fact carries a second FK, so history lives in the fact, not in dimension versions.

q: What is a date (calendar) dimension and why build one as a physical table instead of using SQL date functions on the fact's timestamp?
a: A date dimension is a pre-populated table with one row per calendar day, each carrying a date_key plus rich descriptive attributes like day_of_week, month_name, quarter, fiscal_period, is_holiday, and is_weekend. You build it as a physical table so analysts can filter and group by business attributes (fiscal quarter, holidays) that raw SQL date functions don't know about, so it conforms across every fact for consistent time reporting, and so queries stay simple and fast on a small dimension. It is also the textbook conformed dimension: built once and shared by every fact table. Use an integer key like 20260604 (YYYYMMDD) so it is human-readable, and pre-seed it many years forward so facts never arrive without a matching date row.
fj: I'd just call EXTRACT or DATE_TRUNC on the fact's timestamp in each query.
fs: Date functions can't express fiscal calendars, holidays, or company-specific week logic, and they don't conform across facts. A physical date dimension centralizes those attributes once and is shared by every fact.

q: What is a degenerate dimension, and why does it live in the fact table without its own dimension table?
a: A degenerate dimension is a dimension key that has no attributes of its own, so there is nothing to put in a separate table — examples are order_number, invoice_number, and ticket_id. It sits directly in the fact table as a column because it is already at the fact's grain and any 'lookup table' for it would just duplicate that single value. You keep it because it is operationally vital: it groups all the line items of one order together, ties the warehouse row back to the source transaction, and supports audit and drill-to-detail. Treat it as a real dimension for grouping even though it is physically just a fact column.
fj: An order_number needs its own dim_order table to be a proper dimension.
fs: An order_number has no descriptive attributes — a dim_order table would only repeat that one value. It stays in the fact as a degenerate dimension, used for grouping and drill-to-detail.

q: What is a role-playing dimension? How do you model a fact that references the date dimension three times?
a: A role-playing dimension is a single physical dimension referenced multiple times by the same fact, each reference playing a distinct logical role. An order fact typically carries order_date_key, ship_date_key, and delivery_date_key — all pointing at the one dim_date table. You do not build three date tables; instead you expose three SQL views (or BI aliases) over dim_date with role-specific column names so order_date.month and ship_date.month don't collide. The fact joins each foreign key to the appropriately aliased view, keeping one conformed calendar while letting each date mean something different.
fj: I'd build dim_order_date, dim_ship_date, and dim_delivery_date as three separate tables.
fs: Three physical copies break conformance and triple maintenance. Use one dim_date referenced three times via role-named views or aliases — that's a role-playing dimension.

code.label: one dim_date, three roles
lines: -- alias per role; views rename cols

q: What is SCD Type 0, and when is 'never change this attribute' the correct modeling choice?
a: SCD Type 0 means the attribute is fixed at insert and never updated, even if the source changes — the warehouse retains the original value permanently. It fits durable, point-of-origin facts about an entity: original_credit_score, date_account_opened, or the customer's first acquisition_channel. The intent is the opposite of Type 1: Type 1 overwrites to track 'current truth', while Type 0 deliberately freezes 'truth as of origination'. It is a stated business rule, not laziness, and is often combined with Type 1 or Type 2 columns on the same dimension for attributes that should evolve.
fj: Type 0 just means we forgot to set up change tracking on that column.
fs: Type 0 is a deliberate rule — freeze the original value forever (e.g. original_credit_score, signup_channel). It's not a missing feature; it's the opposite intent from Type 1's overwrite.

q: Kimball vs Inmon — contrast the two data warehouse philosophies and where modern platforms land.
a: Inmon is top-down: build a normalized (3NF) enterprise data warehouse as the single integrated source of truth first, then spin dependent dimensional data marts off it. Kimball is bottom-up: build conformed-dimension star-schema data marts per business process first, integrated through the bus matrix, and the warehouse emerges as their union. Inmon favors integration rigor and long-term consistency at higher upfront cost and slower time-to-value; Kimball favors fast, business-aligned delivery at the risk of integration drift without disciplined conformance. Modern lakehouses are effectively hybrid: a normalized or Data Vault integration layer (Inmon-like) feeding Kimball star marts in the presentation/gold layer.
fj: Kimball and Inmon are the same thing with different vocabulary.
fs: They invert the build order: Inmon is normalized enterprise-warehouse-first then marts; Kimball is conformed star marts first integrated via the bus matrix. Modern stacks blend both across layers.

q: What is a factless fact table? Give the two kinds and how the 'coverage' kind answers 'what didn't happen'.
a: A factless fact table has foreign keys but no numeric measures — the existence of the row is the fact. The first kind records events, like student attendance (one row per student per class per day): you count rows to measure occurrences. The second kind is a coverage table recording conditions or eligibility, like one row per product on promotion per store per day, capturing what could have happened. To answer 'which promoted products did not sell', you take the coverage table and left-anti-join the sales fact: rows present in coverage but absent from sales are the non-events. The signal is that you measure with COUNT(*) rather than SUM(measure).
fj: If a table has no numeric measures it's just a broken or useless fact table.
fs: Factless fact tables are intentional: event tables count occurrences (attendance), and coverage tables record what was possible so you can subtract activity to find 'what didn't happen' (promoted-but-unsold).

q: What is a junk dimension, and what problem does it solve in a fact table full of flags and indicators?
a: A junk dimension collapses several unrelated, low-cardinality flags and indicators — order_status, is_gift, payment_type, ship_priority — into one small dimension whose grain is one row per distinct combination of those values. The fact then carries a single junk_key instead of four separate flag columns, shrinking the fact's width and centralizing the descriptive decode text. You enumerate only the combinations that actually occur (or pre-build the full cross-product if it is small), and add new rows lazily as new combinations appear. It is the tidy alternative to bloating the fact with many low-value columns or, worse, modeling each flag as its own dimension.
fj: Each status and indicator flag should be its own tiny dimension table.
fs: A dozen single-column flag dimensions is over-modeling. A junk dimension folds the unrelated low-cardinality flags into one table keyed by their combination, and the fact carries one junk_key.

q: What is an outrigger dimension, and how is it different from snowflaking?
a: An outrigger is a dimension that is referenced by another dimension rather than directly by the fact — for example dim_customer carrying a date_first_order_key that points at the conformed dim_date. It lets one dimension reuse another's rich attributes (a full calendar) without copying them. The distinction from snowflaking is intent and discipline: snowflaking normalizes a dimension's own attributes across many tables to save space and is generally discouraged, whereas an outrigger is a deliberate, sparing reference to a separate conformed dimension. Kimball's guidance is to use outriggers rarely, because overuse recreates the join complexity that the star schema exists to avoid.
fj: An outrigger dimension is just another word for snowflaking.
fs: An outrigger is a dimension referencing another conformed dimension (dim_customer -> dim_date), used sparingly. Snowflaking normalizes a dimension's own attributes across tables — different intent, and generally discouraged.

q: How does the medallion (bronze/silver/gold) architecture relate to dimensional modeling? Where does the star schema live?
a: Medallion is a layered refinement pattern: bronze holds raw ingested data as-is, silver holds cleaned, conformed, integrated data, and gold holds business-ready consumption models. Dimensional star schemas live in the gold layer, because that layer is the presentation surface for BI, dashboards, and metrics where denormalized fact-and-dimension models give simple, fast queries. A common mature shape is raw in bronze, a Data Vault or 3NF integration model in silver, and Kimball stars in gold. Medallion describes data quality stages, not a modeling technique — it tells you where to apply dimensional modeling, not how to do it.
fj: Medallion architecture replaces star schemas — bronze/silver/gold is the new dimensional model.
fs: Medallion is about refinement stages, not table shape. The star schema lives in the gold layer; silver is often Data Vault or 3NF integration. They compose — medallion says where, Kimball says how.

q: When is a wide One Big Table the right call over a star schema on a modern columnar warehouse, and what do you lose?
a: On columnar engines like Snowflake or BigQuery, pre-joining every dimension into one wide denormalized table can beat a star schema because column pruning reads only the needed columns and there are zero join costs at query time. It shines for a stable, well-known query pattern feeding a high-traffic dashboard or a feature table for ML, where read latency dominates. What you give up is flexibility and governance: a changed dimension attribute forces rebuilding or backfilling the whole table, SCD history is awkward, and conformance across multiple OBTs is hard to guarantee. The pragmatic pattern is a star schema as the authoritative model with OBTs materialized as read-optimized derivatives for specific consumers.
fj: Columnar storage made star schemas obsolete — just build one big table for everything.
fs: OBT wins for a fixed high-traffic query pattern, but you lose flexibility, SCD history, and cross-table conformance. Keep the star as authoritative and materialize OBTs as derivatives.

code.label: OBT derived from the star
lines: -- rebuild when a dim attr changes

q: What are conformed facts (standardized measures), and why do inconsistent units silently corrupt cross-mart comparisons?
a: Conformed facts are measures defined identically across fact tables — same name, same unit, same currency, same business definition — so 'revenue' in the sales mart means exactly what 'revenue' means in the finance mart. Without conformance, one fact stores revenue gross of tax and another net, or one in USD and another in local currency, and a cross-mart comparison produces a plausible but wrong number with no error raised. The fixes are a shared metric definition (often in a semantic layer like dbt MetricFlow or LookML), storing measures in a single canonical unit, and keeping the conversion factor as a dimension attribute rather than baking it into the stored value. Conformed dimensions let you join across marts; conformed facts let you trust the comparison.
fj: If two facts both have a 'revenue' column I can just add them together.
fs: Two 'revenue' columns may use different units, tax treatment, or currency. Conformed facts require one canonical definition and unit; otherwise the cross-mart total is silently wrong.

q: How do you handle a slowly changing fact — a measure that gets corrected or restated after the fact row was loaded?
a: First decide the business semantics: is a late correction an amendment to the original event or a new compensating event? The append-and-reverse pattern keeps the original fact row immutable and inserts an offsetting (negative) row plus the corrected row, so SUM stays additive and the audit trail is preserved — this is how ledgers and restatements are handled. The overwrite pattern updates the measure in place, which is simpler but destroys history and breaks any snapshot already taken. For accumulating snapshots, in-place MERGE is expected because the row is designed to mutate. Choose based on whether downstream consumers need point-in-time reproducibility and an audit trail.
fj: If a measure was wrong I just UPDATE the value in the fact table.
fs: In-place UPDATE destroys history and breaks prior snapshots. For correctable measures, prefer append-and-reverse (negative offset + corrected row) so totals stay additive and the restatement is auditable.

q: A dimension has a ragged, variable-depth hierarchy (org chart, bill-of-materials). How do you model it for roll-up queries?
a: A self-referential parent_key handles a fixed shallow hierarchy but forces recursive CTEs and cannot roll a fact up to every ancestor in one join. The robust pattern is a hierarchy bridge table that materializes every ancestor-descendant pair, storing parent_key, child_key, depth_from_parent, and flags for the top and bottom of the path. A fact joins through the bridge so a single query can aggregate a measure to any level of the org or BOM without recursion. The cost is that the bridge must be rebuilt when the hierarchy changes and you must weight or filter carefully to avoid double-counting shared sub-trees.
fj: Just add a parent_id column and recurse — that covers any hierarchy.
fs: A parent_id self-join needs a recursive CTE and can't roll up to all ancestors cleanly. A hierarchy bridge materializing every ancestor-descendant pair lets one join aggregate to any level of a ragged hierarchy.

code.label: hierarchy bridge roll-up
lines: FROM fct_cost f JOIN bridge_org b

q: Explain SCD Type 7: how does it give both current and historical dimension views, and how does it differ from Type 6?
a: Type 7 keeps a pure Type 2 dimension (versioned rows with effective dating) but has the fact carry two foreign keys to it: the durable/natural key and the surrogate version key. Joining on the surrogate key gives the as-was historical attribute in effect at event time; joining on the durable key against the current row (is_current = true) gives the as-is current attribute — all from the same Type 2 table with no extra columns. The contrast with Type 6 is where the dual view comes from: Type 6 adds an overwritten 'current' column onto each Type 2 row, while Type 7 adds a second key to the fact and resolves current-vs-historical purely by which key you join on. Type 7 keeps the dimension clean; Type 6 keeps the fact narrower.
fj: Type 7 is identical to Type 6 — both just give you current and historical values.
fs: Type 6 adds an overwritten current column to each Type 2 row. Type 7 instead puts two keys (durable + surrogate) on the fact: join on surrogate for as-was, on durable + is_current for as-is.

code.label: two keys on the fact
lines: -- as-was: surrogate key at event time

q: How should a surrogate date key be designed, and is a 'smart' integer like 20260605 a good idea?
a: The date dimension is the one place a meaningful smart integer key is broadly endorsed: a YYYYMMDD integer such as 20260605 is compact, naturally sortable, human-readable in raw fact rows, and lets you partition fact tables on the key directly. This is a deliberate exception to the rule that surrogate keys carry no business meaning, justified because the calendar never rekeys and the encoding is stable forever. Crucially, you still reserve a special key for unknown or not-applicable dates — often -1 or a sentinel like 19000101 — so late-arriving or null dates have a real row to join to and never produce NULL foreign keys. For every other dimension, prefer a meaningless sequence or hash surrogate.
fj: Smart keys are always bad, so the date key should be a meaningless sequence too.
fs: The date dimension is the accepted exception: a YYYYMMDD integer is sortable, readable, and partition-friendly, and the calendar never rekeys. But still reserve a sentinel key for unknown/NA dates.

code.label: smart date key + unknown row
lines: -- late/NULL dates join to -1, not NULL

q: A dimension has grown to 200+ columns mixing stable and rapidly changing attributes. How do you decide whether to split it?
a: First check whether the width is actually a problem: on a columnar warehouse a wide dimension is often fine because unused columns are pruned, so don't split reflexively. Split when distinct concerns collide — most commonly when rapidly changing, low-cardinality attributes force SCD Type 2 versioning that explodes the row count; carve those into a mini-dimension (Type 4) the fact references via a second key. Also split when groups of columns have different ownership, security, or refresh cadence, or when a sub-group is reused by other facts and deserves to be a conformed dimension. The anti-pattern is splitting on width alone, which just reintroduces snowflake joins for no benefit.
fj: A 200-column dimension is obviously too wide — split it up to be safe.
fs: On columnar storage, width alone isn't a reason to split — unused columns are pruned. Split when rapidly-changing attributes force SCD2 row explosion (use a mini-dimension) or when groups differ in ownership/security/cadence.
